title: Argentina Salary Calculator 2026 — Gross to Net Pay | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your net salary in Argentina from gross pay: social security deductions (17%), healthcare, and income tax based on 2026 ARCA/AFIP brackets.
intro: Converting gross salary to net pay in Argentina isn't just subtracting a flat percentage: mandatory social security contributions (11% pension + 3% healthcare + 3% PAMI) and the income tax for earners above the tax-free threshold all come into play. This calculator takes your monthly gross salary, applies the 2026 tax brackets from ARCA (formerly AFIP), and shows you exactly how much you'll receive, how much is deducted, and what percentage that represents. It works for employees under Argentina's Labor Contract Law (LCT 20.744), accounts for dependents for income tax calculations, and is updated whenever the tax-free threshold changes.
keyTakeaway: An Argentine employee loses **17% fixed** in social security contributions (pension + healthcare + PAMI). If your gross exceeds the tax-free threshold (approx. **ARS 2,280,000** in 2026 for a single person without dependents), **income tax** is added on top.
useCases: You're offered a job in Argentina and want to know the actual take-home pay before accepting.
You're negotiating a raise and need to know how much you'll actually receive with the new figure.
You switched from freelance to employment and want to understand the new deductions.
You want to know if your salary exceeds the income tax threshold for budget planning.
You're an employer and want to estimate the net pay an employee will see.
explanation:
## How Net Salary Is Calculated in Argentina

The gross salary is the amount listed at the top of your pay stub before deductions. Two groups of deductions apply: fixed social security contributions and income tax.

### Social Security Contributions (17% fixed)
- **Pension (SIPA)**: 11%
- **Healthcare (Obra Social)**: 3%
- **PAMI (retiree health)**: 3%

These three contributions are paid by every employee under the Labor Contract Law (LCT), regardless of salary level or dependents.

### Income Tax (only for higher earners)
Fourth-category income tax applies **only if your gross salary exceeds the Tax-Free Threshold (MNI)** per month. The MNI adjusts for inflation and increases with each declared dependent.

### Simplified Formula
```
Net = Gross - (Gross x 0.17) - Income Tax
```

## 2026 Tax-Free Threshold (Approximate)

| Family Situation | Monthly MNI (approx.) |
|---|---|
| Single, no children | ARS 2,280,000 |
| + 1 dependent | ARS 2,680,000 |
| + 2 dependents | ARS 3,080,000 |
| + 3 dependents | ARS 3,480,000 |
| + 4 or more dependents | ARS 3,880,000 |

> The MNI is updated quarterly. Verify the exact figure at [ARCA (ex AFIP)](https://www.arca.gob.ar/ganancias).

## Understanding Argentine Payroll

Argentina's payroll system has several unique features:
- **Aguinaldo (SAC)**: a 13th-month salary paid in two installments (June and December)
- **Union dues**: vary by industry (typically 2-3% of gross)
- **Non-taxable benefits**: some allowances don't count toward social security
- **Employer contributions**: on top of your deductions, employers pay an additional ~26% in contributions

## What This Calculator Does NOT Include

- **Union dues**: vary by collective bargaining agreement (2-3%)
- **Court-ordered deductions**: child support, garnishments
- **Salary advances** or loans from employer
- **Non-taxable bonuses** (may add 8-20% without social charges)
- **Aguinaldo (SAC)**: paid separately in June and December
- **Vacation pay**: calculated differently

## Common Mistakes

1. **Thinking 17% is the only deduction**: if you earn above ARS 2.3M gross, you must add income tax.
2. **Not declaring dependents**: each dependent raises the MNI. You need to file form F.572 with HR.
3. **Confusing net salary with actual spending money**: your bank account also gets hit by debit/credit tax (0.6%).
4. **Using base salary vs total taxable income**: if you receive allowances, overtime, or bonuses, the taxable gross is higher.

faq.q: What is gross salary vs net salary?
faq.a: **Gross salary** is the total amount agreed with the employer before deductions. **Net salary** (or 'take-home pay') is what you actually receive in your bank account after subtracting pension contributions, healthcare, PAMI, and — if applicable — income tax.

faq.q: Why are 17% deducted in social security?
faq.a: It's the sum of three mandatory contributions in Argentina: **11%** to pension (SIPA), **3%** to healthcare (Obra Social), and **3%** to PAMI (retiree health system). All employees under the LCT pay this, regardless of salary level.

faq.q: When does income tax apply to my salary?
faq.a: Only if your monthly gross exceeds the **Tax-Free Threshold (MNI)**. In 2026, the MNI for a single person without dependents is approximately ARS 2,280,000 per month. Each dependent you declare raises it by ~ARS 400,000.

faq.q: Does this calculator include aguinaldo and vacation pay?
faq.a: No. This calculates the regular monthly salary. For aguinaldo, use the dedicated aguinaldo calculator. For vacation pay, the calculation method is different.

faq.q: How do I declare dependents to lower my income tax?
faq.a: You need to file form **SiRADIG (F.572 web)** annually or whenever your situation changes. You can include: spouse, children under 18, disabled family members, and deductible payments (private health insurance, rent, donations, etc.).

faq.q: Are deductions the same in all Argentine provinces?
faq.a: Yes. Social security contributions (17%) and income tax are federal. The only territorial difference may be **union dues**, which vary by collective bargaining agreement and province.

faq.q: Does this calculation apply to freelancers (monotributo)?
faq.a: No. Freelancers under the monotributo regime pay a fixed monthly fee that includes pension, healthcare, and a simplified tax. This calculator is for employees under the Labor Contract Law (LCT).

example.title: Example: ARS 2,500,000 gross salary, no dependents
